-- 2 of 60 --

Welcome to Butts in Seats and Bucks in the Bank. If you’re serious about succeeding in the
seminar business, then buckle your seat belts and get ready because Dan Kennedy and Bill
Glazer, two of the most successful and profitable seminar marketers on the planet, are finally
spilling the beans on how to put butts in seats and bucks in the bank…including their Closely-
Guarded Secrets for Filling Large Rooms and Creating BIG PAYDAYS with Seminars in ANY
Economy!!
With nearly 50 years of collective experience and over $100 MILLION generated from Seminars
attended by over 1 MILLION people – including their own perpetually sold out and highly
anticipated annual seminars, Dan Kennedy and Bill Glazer are the envy of the Seminar
Marketing World. And here’s a golden opportunity to peek “behind the curtains” to see exactly
how they are able to consistently extract MAXIMUM profits from their events.
Dan and Bill share their profitable insights monthly in the famous No BS Marketing Letter – the
largest and most read publication of its kind. Dan also shares specific information on marketing
to the affluent – those least and last affected by the roller coaster economy and least likely to
make buying decisions based on price – in his monthly Marketing to the Affluent newsletter.
If you’re not already receiving these monthly newsletters, you’re invited to take a FREE Test-
Drive of Glazer-Kennedy’s elite GOLD Membership. To claim $633.91 worth of pure money-
making information including 2 issues of the No BS Marketing Letter, visit DanKennedy.com.
You’ll also have the opportunity to upgrade your FREE Trial Membership to receive the
Marketing to the Affluent Letter.
For information on more business and wealth building resources from Dan & Bill, visit the
Internet’s only official source for the latest news and information about upcoming events and
resources including the current Web Deal of the Week at DanKennedy.com.
And now, here’s Dan & Bill…

-- 3 of 60 --

Dan Kennedy
Dan: We are supposed to talk about it, so we will talk about it, putting butts
in seats. And you have another session of this tomorrow night, so my part is
I thought I would talk with you some about the psychology of seminar
attendance. Not so much the mechanics of marketing the seminar, although
I'll show you some marketing pieces, but really the mindset of the buyer, of
the prospect, of the attendee, why people do, why they don't, why so few of
them register, why we don't get better results than we do. What's going on
that we can affect, that we can manipulate, that drives people to either
register or not register to attend an event.
How many of you in here now as part of your business put on some kind of
an event? Seminar conference -- oh, everybody. Okay. How many of you
think you're getting all the results you can get? Oh, okay. All right.
How many in here are seeing declining response in the last couple of years,
or higher costs to put the butt in the seat? Okay. A few of you. That's sort
of the norm if that makes you feel any better. It shouldn't, but sometimes
the fact that others are suffering.
I've had one client for the past three years, he's in a particular industry, that
literally has been in just free fall, and so really the only thing I've been able
to do on most of the coaching calls is just tell him about somebody else's
misery and make him feel better. There's really nothing to be done to the
system.
So that actually is the norm. In the last couple of years the seminar industry,
if you will, if there is such a thing, most of the people I'm in contact with
have been fighting rising costs, slightly declining response. And many of
them for ten years thought they were geniuses at this, and discovered that
they were benefiting from certain conditions in the market place, so now
they're challenged by certain conditions in the market place.
But I think generally people when they set out to market a seminar or a
convention or a conference, they make the same basic mistake that we do
when we set out to market products. They start and they totally focus on the
thing. How many days is it? Who's speaking? What are the activities?
What are we going to call it? And they don't really give enough thought to
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	1© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 4 of 60 --

what's inside the head of the people who they want to attend. So that's
where we're going to spend most of our allotted time tonight.
The first mistake I think is this. People underestimate the enormous
resistance there is to attending. There's almost an assumption on many
people's part that their herd is just dying to get on the bus and head out to
the next seminar or the next conference or to their annual event -- whoever
back there is running the light switch, I took the thing off -- the doohickey,
and so we should now be seeing me. There we go.
And so they start out with the assumption that people are just dying to come
to another event, and for a time that may have been more true than it is now
in some niches. I do a lot of work with people, for example info marketers
in the chiropractic and the dental professions, and literally the clutter there is
so extreme. I know some of you in the room work those industries. I mean,
if you're a chiropractor and you like seminars, there's at least ten every
weekend, maybe more that you can go to. And then there's some during the
week. And then there's some on evenings. And we're not even counting
webinars and teleseminars and all.
So you could spend your life in them, and actually if you camped out in the
Anaheim Hilton and you were a chiropractor, you actually would never have
to leave. You could just -- because there's enough seminars in the Anaheim
Hilton that as one finished for chiropractors there'd be another one starting,
and you could just go from one to the other. Every once in a while if there
was a break there'd be some human potential metaphysical woo-woo thing
that could fill in the three days in California, so you could just live in the
Hilton and go from seminar to seminar to seminar.
And in many cases, in many industries, that's the environment that we're in.
In others, there's not so much clutter, but still because you go and you are a
regular goers as well as a giver, you don't really sometimes think about how
most people may not like going to the airport, or they may not want to be in
a hotel, or they may have real problems being away from family or business,
and so forth. So there's this enormous resistance in place when we start the
process of trying to get somebody to disrupt their life and move from
wherever they are to this event that we want them to be in.
So I thought I would talk to you about all of the different kinds of resistance
that get in the way, and sort of give you a checklist to deal with because you
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	2© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 5 of 60 --

really have to address all of them. So the first one is embarrassment and
shame. So often we are trying to get people to come to an event where they
are our customer, they are our student, they are our client, and they are
woefully unsuccessful and they have implemented nothing that they have
gotten from us prior to this event. And this is often the case because the
seminar is often where the light bulb goes on that finally gets them to do
something.
So this guy is sitting in his office, Rory Fatt and I used to talk about this all
the time when I did a lot of marketing for Rory's events for restaurant
owners. I said, so we got this restaurant owner and he's sitting in his office,
and our whiz-bang seminar brochure is in his hands. And right up above
him there's a shelf, and on the shelf is a bunch of notebooks. About six blue
ones, six white ones, six yellow ones, six maroon, some of them got shrink
wrap still on them, a bunch of them don't have shrink wrap on them, but
they got a lot of stuff. The blue ones have 365 ways to market your
restaurant. The yellow ones have how to create a referral culture in your
restaurant, on and on and on, and guess what? He hasn't used one damned
thing in any of those notebooks.
Now you might think that then that's an economic issue. Gee, I haven't
gotten any value out of all this stuff I already bought. Why do I want to go
to a conference? That's not as big as he doesn't want to be the guy at the
conference who hasn't done anything, and be surrounded by people, because
he thinks of course he's the only guilty party. And if you do your job right,
that's what he should think, however erroneous that may be. So he doesn't
want to be the only one sitting in the room like the only kid who comes to
class who hasn't done his homework. That's an unpleasant environment to
put yourself in.
So this embarrassment about not having implemented, not having used what
he has, not having been as successful. So what do we show him when we
invite him to a seminar? What's one of the things we show him? Of course,
everyone else's success. We show him testimonials and success stories and
case histories about everybody who's kicking butt and taking names. He's
just seen pages of them, watched a DVD of them, listened to them, and now
he thinks he's going to be the only guy in the room, and God forbid he
should get called on. I haven't been doing nothing. So, he doesn't want to
be there, right? So that's one type of resistance.
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	3© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 6 of 60 --

Another type of embarrassment and shame resources is money. A person's
got money trouble, and they are not going to be able to stay in the host hotel.
They're not going to be able to go to meal functions. They're going to want
to buy stuff. They know people are going to try and sell them stuff. They're
going to want the stuff that people are trying to sell them, and they're not
going to be able to buy it. All right? And so they don't want to put
themselves in that environment.
I can't tell you over the years the number of people who have been to an
event of mine, the vast majority of them have then had some transformation
in their circumstances. Not all, but have been to an event of mine and have
confessed at some point that when they get turned loose for meals, they're
up in the room and brought peanut butter, non-perishable food from home
because they have no money for the restaurant. They're staying down the
road in the Motel 6 and hiking up the driveway to the hotel. I can think of
one who was sleeping in his car the whole four days at a $5,000 seminar.
I can't tell you the number of times that this has happened with doctors, with
not just shoeless, toothless, but professional people because they are
sometimes broke too, actually more than we would think. Most people
think everybody in some other field than theirs that everybody's making a
lot of money, and that's not how the pyramid sorts itself. But so there's that.
I don't want to go there, even though I really need to be there, and I really
want to be there, but what if I have to pick up a tab in the bar? So I can't
hang out in a bar. So that's going on.
Then there's feeling intimidated, feeling like they're going to be in over their
head, right? So who -- there we go. So that's discomfort. I haven't read my
book, so I'm not going to know the language. Everybody's going to be
ahead of me. I'm going to feel stupid even if I don't look stupid because I'm
not really going to understand what's going on. Right? That's another kind
of resistance.
Some people then immediately anticipate negative emotions. One of the
first sort of specialized seminars I ever did was for my peers in speaking in
1979, I think, and a fairly prominent member of the National Speakers
Association speaker organized this thing, rounded up 20 people who each
paid the fee to get me to come to San Francisco and do this three-day
business training for them. And at the end of the three days she came up to
me and said I have never been more depressed in my entire life than I am
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	4© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 7 of 60 --

right now. This is what you have done to me in these three days. And I
said, “gee -- she's the one that organized the thing.”
It certainly really wasn't my intent to be -- I actually thought I was
motivational. And she said, no, she said, I get it. That this is like a real
business and you've got to run it like a real business, and there's 512 moving
parts, and I don't like that, and it's made me feel horrible because I only have
one of the 512 moving parts, and I really don't want the other 511. And so
she is not the only person on the planet who has these reactions. And if they
have had that reaction at event A, and now six months later we're trying to
get them to come to event B, they anticipate having that reaction again. And
what most people do when they know they're going to have an adverse
emotional experience is they do their best to avoid it.
Let's see. How many guys in here are married? What do you do when she
says we need to talk? Anything but talk, right? Anything. Why? Because
odds are it's not gonna be good. Those conversations are usually not, you
know it's been a month since I told you everything I liked about what you're
doing as a husband, and I just want to sit you down and give you a praise
session. See that. Depending on how you care to count, I've been married
for 22 years, 18 years, it just depends whether you count the divorce or not,
however you count, never have I had that conversation one single time.
And I know I will die without ever having it, but I have heard, we need to
talk.
So when someone anticipates a negative adverse emotional situation, what
do they do? They get away from it. In this case, they don't register in the
first place. What else? They'll be confused. So some people fear that
they're going to be confused and distracted from what they're doing by all
that they're shown that they're not doing. They don't want to be confused, so
they don't come.
Other people anticipate that they're going to get all jazzed up and then
they're going to crash a few days later. They've had that experience in the
seminar room all pumped up and excited and invigorated, and pages of
ideas, and little post-it notes stuck on their toes when they go home, and
then five or six days later, they're blew hew right back to where they were.
And so that wasn't a really good experience and so that pops up into their
mind now, again, and they don't want that, so they don't register. Another
type of resistance.
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	5© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 8 of 60 --

Poor resistance with the host or the marketer. So I cannot tell you also the
number of times over the years that somebody has come to me and hired me
to write copy for their first conference seminar boot camp. And I have
asked questions before taking their money about what's your relationship
with your customers that we're going to send this wonderful announcement
of your event to. Oh, they love me. They love me. I've got a great
relationship. And then the mail goes and nobody registers.
And then if you put somebody on the phone to start calling their customers,
guess what you find out? 33% of them don't even remember who the guy is.
33% of them are thoroughly pissed off about something that was either done
or not done to them six months or six years ago that has never been
rectified. Right? And 33% of them are even madder.
And then they can't figure out why they're not filling a room. So there's a
foundation to this before you can get somebody to put their butt in the room,
there's a relationship foundation normally that has to be in place. Putting
them in cold is a very, very difficult proposition, and so sometimes this is an
alarm bell that then causes a change in behavior about how the customers
are handled. Sunday morning at the herd-building thing, I'm really talking
about the care and feeding of the herd.
And so one kind of resistance is I don't have a very good relationship with
this guy and now he wants me to give him $3,000 and fly across the country
and sit in a room for three days and listen to him, and I don't like him. I
don't believe him. He's ignored me ever since I bought X from him, and
now all of a sudden he's shown up and wants money. Poor customer service
will undermine now asking them to come to a seminar, maybe more than
anything else.
Money is another form of resistance. They may actually have an inability to
buy. Now that ability can be cured if they are sufficiently motivated
because everybody can go get money from somewhere, but they may have a
legitimate inability to buy. They may have difficulty securing permission to
buy. So here's a big one. Almost everybody has to get permission. If
they're married, they have to get permission. Now, a lot of them will tell
you they don't, but they do. They have to go ask. Different limits, for some
if it's over $10, for some if it's over $100, but just about everybody if it's
over a couple of thousand.
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	6© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 9 of 60 --

I once, while married, bought a house without having a conversation about
it, or showing it to her. It had a fireplace. I knew she wanted a fireplace.
That was my argument. Not a good move. Not good. To the point that you
have to understand, if it had been her dream house, you're still going to lose
this battle now over this decision. So everybody has somebody, business
associate, partner, staff, mentor, or spouse that they have to actually go get
permission. Now they might never phrase it that way, they might not even
think about it that way, but they are going and having to tell the story to
somebody. Hey, I'm taking X out of the till here, and I'm going to be gone
for four days, and I'm going to be...
This happens to be by the way, personally, why I am not a big fan ever of
putting an event in Vegas. There's a number of reasons actually. One's a
logistical operational thing. All of the kind of events we do and all of the
kind of events my clients do, we sell stuff while people are there. So it
always seems to be a bad idea to be competing with Las Vegas for dollars.
Right? They have booze, they got hookers, we got tapes.
See to me that's just never seems smart, but beyond that I'm well aware that
when Charlie goes to Barbara to tell the story, I need to go to this seminar,
which is going to cost more than I made last month, in order to improve X Y
and Z, and I'm going to be gone for four days, it adds to his difficulty when
she says, where is the seminar? And he says, Las Vegas. What she hears
when he says Las Vegas is what I said two and a half minutes ago. She
hears booze and hookers. That's what just went through her head. And so
we just made his job more difficult. Vice versa, we made her job more
difficult if she's the one going. So it's never made a lot of sense to me. But
people do have to go tell this story to somebody about the thing they are
going to go to.
And so now they not only have their psychological resistance, they have this
other person's psychological resistance. So we have to equip them to be
able to deal with that. We have the gees, another seminar reaction. Right?
So for all sorts of reasons, theirs and that other person, well, you could skip
one. Well, this is a year money's tight, the economy's not good, all the
reasons that you might hear about another seminar.
We have anticipated what I would call specific negative experiences.
Right? So one is the whole thing's just going to be a place where they try
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	7© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 10 of 60 --

and sell me a bunch of stuff, and they're going to put a lot of pressure on me
to buy stuff, so I don't want to put myself in that environment. So when you
go into a store and the clerk comes up and says, "may I help you?" What do
you say? No. Why do you say no? You say, no, I'm just looking. Why?
You don't want to be sold. You don't want to be put under pressure. And
you say it even if you could use help. Right? You could actually use help.
I did it myself just the other day, it's like a reflex action. I go to a bookstore
to find some Bob the Builder stuff, never mind why, and I'm in the kid's
section and the clerk comes over and says, “can I help?”, and I'm lost. I'm
finding out that there's Thomas the Train in there, but I can't find a Bob the
Builder, and can I help you? And reflexively I say, no, no, no, no I'm fine.
And I could have used help. All right? But we react that way. Same deal
here. So even though people may want to go find resources, there's still a
“gee, I don't know if I want to put myself in that position.” Right? So
really, the whole thing is about selling stuff, so that's an anticipated negative
experience.
Another anticipated negative experience is I'm too smart for the room. I'm
more advanced than most the people that are going to be there. If I hang out
with them I'm going to be teaching for free. I'm going to be in a room with
a bunch of dumb dogs. I don't want to be in a room with a bunch of dumb
dogs. Right? Poor environment if they've had that kind of experience. Gee,
the seating's going to be bad. I'm not going to be able to see. I'm not going
to be able to hear. My butt's going hurt. My back, chairs, I don't sleep good
in hotel beds. Okay?
There's a fear of workshop thing that keeps people from registering. Fear of
workshop means that they're going to be forced to do workshopy things. So
like the okay, now everybody turn to their -- rub the shoulders of the person
in front of you, and then turn to your neighbor and tell them five deep dark
secrets about yourself that nobody knows, and you have seven minutes, and
do the exercise. Right? So some people don't like that. I happen to be one
of them. I'm not a group participation kind of guy. So some people just
stay away because they fear that sort of thing's going to happen to them.
There's legitimate disinterest. They're not convinced of the personal
relevance of the subject matter to them. They're not convinced that they
couldn't get the information easier, cheaper, faster. The speakers are going
to be giving book reports; I could just stay home and read the book. They're
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	8© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 11 of 60 --

unconvinced that the speaker is something really special. There's
terminology some -- I've heard it from more than one seminar goers, I'm not
going to that conference because all the speakers are the usual whores. By
the way, he meant to include me in the group. What the customer means by
that is it's the same speakers on that subject matter that are at 18 conferences
during the year. They're all showing up all together, almost in the same
order on 18 different seminars, and gee, I don't want to go see them again.
That sort of legitimate disinterest, so that can be in the way.
We're almost done. There's time. So legitimately, I can't be away for X
days. My business is in such disarray and dysfunction that if I'm gone for
three days there's no point in going back because it will be back by the
fourth day. Well, a lot of business people live in exactly that environment,
or their personal life is in that situation. The price of the time is too high.
Professionals for example, have very high chair side value. People who
make high income from doing personal work, cosmetic dentist, cosmetic
surgeon. I think about it myself. So it costs me because I could work every
day writing copy or consulting at home if I wanted to, so I'm down $18,800
any day I'm not home. So it's costing me $18,800 the minute I walk out the
door and go some place else instead of staying home and working. So you
multiply that times three days, that's a chunk of change. That'll take care of
the vet bill at the barns for a month.
Too busy to implement more, why go get more? The story about the farmer
who didn't want to come to the farm bureau meeting because he's not
farming half as smart as he already knows to now, so why go get more. I
haven't used what I got the last time. Somebody guilts them, so you've
already spent X amount of time. You're never home now. You're never
with us now, so there's that going on.
And finally now, there's some resistance amongst a lot of people to travel.
Rory, at one time, and Craig Proctor too, is Craig in the room? You here?
There you are. So Craig, for those of you who don't know, is like the guru
to real estate agents, all 12 of them that are left in North America. At one
time -- because Craig's from Canada -- at one time, both Rory and Craig had
their boot camps in Canada, and they finally moved them south into the
United States. Well, they go back and forth to Canada all the time. They
don't think it's any big deal. And by the way, it isn't any big deal, but for a
lot of people they think it's a big deal. This is a distant foreign land. They
think there's moose running around up there in the streets, snow on the
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	9© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 12 of 60 --

ground, they're going to need mukluk boots, and I don't have a passport. If
you travel a lot, you don't think anything of it, but people who don't travel a
lot -- the last stat I saw, and I think it was a 2009 stat, it is still either 26 or
27% of the American population has never been on an airplane. They've
never been on an airplane. They're still standing there going nah.
Now to be fair a bunch of them are in a bible belt, but I mean seriously,
they're standing there going oh, no, uh-huh. 28% of the people. A big
percentage of people do not travel regularly. They don't do this as a normal
thing. So now I've given you a list, now think about this, a long list of all
the resistance that's in place -- now not everybody has all of it of course.
Hardly anybody would have all of it. But just about everybody we're trying
to convince to come to a seminar has some of it going on at any one given
moment in time inside their head.
If you look at most seminar marketing and most conference marketing
literature or websites, it doesn't address this at all. It acts as if this stuff
doesn't exist. It just starts selling the event. It begins with selling the event,
it ends with selling the event, it does not acknowledge that any of this stuff
is in place. But, it's in place, some of it, with just about everybody. So
when you craft both your event itself and the marketing of the event, you
want to take into account all of it, and you want to address as much of it as
you possibly can.
Now briefly, quickly as an aside, some people have the philosophy that why
raise negative thoughts that may not exist. Well, A, because they exist. So
pretending they don't exist is nuts. But beyond that, we're not selling in an
interactive environment. If we were, if we're making a sale one on one,
that's a different story because now you're going to raise some of your own
objections, but at a distance we're not going to have that interactivity, so
you've got to cover every base. Trying to pretend it's not there is a losing
proposition.
So I'll show you a few examples out of the files, some things where many of
the kinds of resistance we just talked about were dealt with in one way or
another. This is a piece for Rory for Restaurant Marketing Systems, and
first of all what's significant is that we get to the resistance very early, so in
this case this is page 1 and so I've gone right to the money resistance issue
by citing very specific results tied to testimonials and telling stories. So
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	10© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 13 of 60 --

this one's a Two-Week, $37,000 Cash Flow Surge. This one's $14,000 In
Profit. This one's 75 New Customers in a Week.
So that's an answer to me for just about any kind of money resistance there
is, I don't have it. Well, but if I knew this then I wouldn't have that problem.
So I can't afford not to go.
It's also an answer to the person the story has to be told to. So now I've got
to go tell Helen that I'm going to be absent from the restaurant for three
days, I'm going to spend money we don't have, and I'm going to go to this
thing, and Helen's going to stay back here and hold down the fort, which is
another unpopular idea, but I at least got some ammo. Helen, we need a
cash flow surge, $37,000 in Two Weeks. We need new customers, 75 New
Customers in a Week.
So I'm dealing with that resistance, and let me show you this little margin
note. So this has to do with both the money and the I won't implement, I've
got stuff I haven't -- little margin note. I'm going to give you the ad and the
strategy from Joe's Two-Week Promotion, which produced $13,500 in a
week. So you're not just going to come to a seminar, you're going to go
home with this thing in your hand ready to use that turns in to money. So
I'm addressing their resistance.
This is another money resistance thing. This is actually Tom Orent’s. Tom
may be here too I think. This is 97.4% case acceptance, which in dentistry
case acceptance translates to money. And, this line here is pretty important,
no salesmanship required. Now that's a kind of resistance I didn't
specifically name, but so every dentist in the world would like a 97.4% case
acceptance, but most dentists don't want to sell. So when you say the
benefit, what's all the negative stuff that comes in somebody's mind? You
better answer the negative. In this case, the first thing the dentist thinks of
yeah, but to get 97.4% case acceptance I've got to be a super salesman and
I've got to knock the patient to the ground, take the wallet out of their
pocket, beat them up, scream at them, put them in a room, bring in the sales
manager, etc. And so we've got to tell them, no salesmanship required.
Now this is a good one. So “Not Another Seminar,” it says “this is no time
for any ordinary dental seminar.” Not the usual whores. It doesn't say that,
I'm reminding you of the kind of resistance. No, the copy is “I have
personally gone underground these recent months to find and ferret out the
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	11© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 14 of 60 --

not usual people inside our profession who are in these times achieving
extraordinary, mind blowing, ta da ta da, to uncover doctors with really
fresh new different even radical ideas about practice, and for the most part
they keep to themselves and keep quiet about it.” So that's an answer to the
not the usual whores.
This is another “Not Another Seminar,” same piece. The subhead says,
“skip the usual dental seminars and save your money. So please do not
schlep off to any ordinary dental seminars that are in truth the same as the
previous year's dental seminars with little more than their names or a few
faces changed. Do not waste your time or money revisting the past with the
present label stuck over the top. This is what you're thinking, isn't it? Gee,
think how much money can be saved by not going to seminars this year.
Well, you're right and I agree, the last darned thing you need is another yada
yada.” There's three paragraphs of it. Because it is what they're thinking.
And so we have got to emphatically convince them that this seminar is not
another seminar.
Regarding getting permission. Big guarantee. Big bold guarantee. So
again, when Helen goes to Charlie, or Charlie goes to Helen and has to tell
the story of going off to the seminar, well what if it's a big waste of time?
Well, now Charlie or Helen have an answer. So this is a big guarantee. The
most daring, brazen and bold guarantee of my entire 26-year career. If by
the end of day one you find me guilty of failing to uncover, reveal, or hand
over to you a real bonafide underground secrets, ta da ta da, if you find me
guilty you may demand whatever you deem fair. A full fee refund or a full
fee refund plus your documented airfare and lodging, or even double your
fee refunded plus documented airfare and lodging. Find me guilty and you
pick the punishment that you deem just, etc. etc. etc. So this is an answer to
the reader and it's ammo for the reader when he has to go and tell the story
to someone else.
Personal relevance. You've seen this piece. This is pretty recent. So there's
never been a more important time. The real important thing is the or. How
to reinvent your information marketing business or enter this highly
profitable industry. Here's an answer to not the usual whores. Guidance
from legitimate experts who do not just teach. What's the caption up here?
The richest, most successful info marketers in the world, and those most
serious about entering the field and watching their own information
businesses.

-- 15 of 60 --

Here's “Not Another Seminar” by means of the total experience. You've all
seen some version of this. We did it I think three years in a row for Rory.
We had a better your best contest, gave away a car. Extreme restaurant
makeover, somebody's going to qualify for that. Are you going to go home
in the convertible? A lot of stuff going on. So here's the extreme lightening
round, success case history interview panel, extreme networking, only such
contest in the industry. I call that three-ring circus. There's a reason they
don't do a one-ring circus. They've all come in a tent, got to be more going
on. So over here, you've got elephants, over here you've got trapeze artists;
over here you've got clowns getting out of the car, the Chevy Volt. You've
got to have a lot of stuff going on.
Here's again, telling them it's not, so here's five ways this academy is
profoundly different than any seminar, workshop, boot camp, or other
training event you've ever invested in. And then there's five reasons why it's
different. Anticipated negative experience. So here's paragraph says, Read
this paragraph if you're a skeptic, skip it if you're not. You might be
skeptical and question whether there really is anything Kennedy you haven't
heard umpteen times before, anything actually held back until now, that
would be a fool's skepticism. And then there's a whole paragraph
convincing them that there's stuff they haven't heard yet, and I haven't
shown them.
By the way, this block of copy came as a result of having the first draft read
by some potential attendees and having them give me some feedback, and
one of the pieces of feedback was, I don't really believe that you held
anything back. And I've been hanging around for umpteen years and I know
everything you've got. So if it's in the mind of one, it's probably in the mind
of others, and so now we've got to deal with that because he's anticipating a
negative experience, hearing stuff he already knows and has heard before,
and he thinks he's too smart for the room. Those things are all on the list I
gave you.
This is another denial. So this is, the subhead is, “Not Another Damned
Seminar.” And then regarding implementation, right, because they think
they're not going to go do anything. We're engineering this multi-day
training so that you make definite definitive decisions as we proceed and are
given actual examples of every key strategy to model and depart with a
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	13© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 16 of 60 --

complete transformation plan including short-term, medium-term, and long-
term implementation in days and weeks.
So we're telling them they're not going home to implement, they're going
home already having implemented. The best version of that I ever saw,
unfortunately it was impossible to fulfill, but it was amazing. Gary Halbert,
how many of you know who Gary is? So Gary Halbert, I spoke at all of his
$7,000 per person marketing seminars in Key West, so I was invited sort of
in the last minute to go down and speak at one and I was horrified when I
got there and realized what the promise was to a room nearly this large.
The promise was you were going to come to your three-day seminar, the
seminar was nothing but hot seats, so everybody was coming up and getting
a quick fix of their business with different experts on the panel depending
on what your thing was, and then those experts were going in a room with
you writing a full-page ad for you and you were leaving the seminar with
the ad written, typeset, formatted, and ready to place in the media. Well, it's
a hell of a promise, isn't it? And it was fabulous.
However, by the fourth person you're so far behind, I mean it's just
completely impossible, implausible, no sane person should have believed it
in the first place. But, it was a great answer to this go home empty-handed
problem. No, your ad's going to be done, it's going to be formatted. It was
fabulous. It was amazing.
One of the big negative experiences, it's going to be another seminar, it's
going to be an ordinary seminar, and frankly, it's not going to be any fun.
It's going to be dull; it's going to be boring. And people remember are
willing to tolerate almost anything, but not having a good time. So a lot of
what we did for Rory was all about having a good time.
So this piece is One Step in a Sequence for Non-Registrants. It's got a big
nasty looking monkey on the front of it and the headline is, "Let's stop
monkeying around. I'm very disappointed you are not registered for the
upcoming greatest show on earth boot camp. I've sent along an inflatable
monkey." And so there's an inflatable monkey in here. "Since you have
decided to stay home I imagine you might want a circus monkey of your
own to keep you company while you're home alone, mired in your day to
day routine while more than 500 of the most exciting wildly prospering
restaurant owners from all over America are whooping it up in sunny

Phoenix meeting and exchanging ideas with each other, winning prizes,

genius P.T. Barnum, getting solid “how to make more information from the
greatest lineup of experts I've assembled in six years.” While we're sharing
all that experience you can be home with your inflatable monkey."
And then there's a page that looks like this. "Are you still sure you want to
stay home with your monkey?" See, it sort of makes it a tonality statement,
doesn't it? That besides laying it on them about staying home, but it kind of
makes a tonality statement.
These are testimonials, you've seen a piece like this before where there's a list
of everybody who's already registered, but then each one of the testimonials
that we chose deals with a different type of resistance that was on my
resistance list. So this one, "I won't make this mistake again. I purchased the
program in 2001 before there was a boot camp to go to and had a hard time
getting started. I was confused. I was having trouble implementing the
strategies. And so I made the wrong decision and decided not to go to the
first ever boot camp and I thought about it often from June of 2002 until
January when I had a chance to go." Then it's, "Next time I still made the
wrong decision and passed on it. All the reasons are excuses. I guess I
thought I knew enough." And then finally we get to the punch line of “finally
went and discovered how little they knew and revolutionized.”
This is just an answer to small town. So this is, "Pity the restaurateur with all
the answers. I was one of them." And this guy goes on to tell about how
successful he already was, but he was working too hard and didn't know it
could be easy. "I was leery about coming and spending that kind of money
because we'd been in business for 21 years and were very successful."
So, every one of the testimonials in this piece was chosen to address a
different kind of resistance on the list, and the headline above each
testimonial let you find the one that matched up with your particular kind of
resistance.
Three-ring circus. You get a sense of it. "So what's happening at the..." This
was the magic one. We dressed Rory up like a magician; I think Dave Deiss
or the other night Dave was at this one. So again, there's all sorts of thing
going on. There are celebrities. There's a car give away. We pulled a rabbit
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	15© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 18 of 60 --

out of a hat panel. There's a top secret Mrs. X. There's Rory doing
something. There's magical networking. There's an optional restaurant tour.
The night before the boot camp everybody gets on buses and goes around to
fascinating restaurants. There are all kinds of moving parts. All sorts of stuff
going on so that clearly this is not an ordinary “sit in a room and be lectured
to” seminar.
You guys probably all remember the best SuperConferenceSM piece of all the
ones we've done was the one for the year we had Gene Simmons, and again --
so this picture's proof right? .There's a ton of people there, therefore there'll
be a ton of people there, and therefore you should be there. Again, there are a
lot of moving parts. There was a car give away that year. There was of
course Simmons. There was a seminar within a seminar.
You've got to make your speakers relevant. So remember, one of the kinds of
resistance is personal relevance, right? So you want a celebrity speaker
because people like to have their picture taken with a celebrity. They like to
see a celebrity. They like to hear from a celebrity, but you've got to go out of
your way to make the celebrity relevant, otherwise you're leaving half the
value behind. So when I write copy I'm always about how are we going to
take this guy, and so in many cases, like in Rory's we had Joe Vitale speak.
Joe has a book called There's a Customer Born Every Minute, which is about
how P.T. Barnum would market your business. So I made a piece of course,
how would P.T. Barnum promote your restaurant? Because these guys are
restaurant owners, and if you don't say restaurant, they don't get it.
When we had Famous Amos speak, we took all the Famous Amos books,
took stuff out of it, rewrote it and made a little book that went out with the
promotional package. This is kind of interesting, it’s a miracle the client
didn't get sued. So this was for the circus year, so this is a Dr. Seuss kind of
brochure. This whole thing is done in Dr. Seuss style presenting everything
that's going on at the seminar. I think this is the one where we shot Rory out
of a cannon at the beginning of the seminar too. No. Now I'm deadly serious.
One of them we shot him out of a cannon, another one we had him ride in on
an elephant.
So here's Famous Amos. We took Famous Amos's book cover and then we
rewrote the book inside and we added restaurant testimonials, and made 12
principles of how you can build your restaurant, making the speaker relevant
because that's an issue. So if you go back to the list of all of the kinds of
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	16© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 19 of 60 --

resistance, and you may have extra ones that apply to your folks that don't
necessarily apply to everybody, the correct approach to this is to figure out
every kind of resistance in the way of their registration. And then address it
in as many different ways as you can and pay at least as much attention to,
give at least as much emphasis to, what's going on in their head as they try not
to buy. Do you understand?
Even the ones, except the very easy ones, almost everybody who's marketing
to or hope to have a relationship again and again and again, there's a relatively
small percentage who know they're coming. You could send them a one-page
fax and say here's when the event is, sign here and you'd get them. But
setting them aside, the most important thing I can tell you in summation of all
this is even the ones who are your pretty good customers are starting out
trying not to register. That's where they're at because there are a lot of
reasons not to. So they're starting trying not to register. You need to start
where they start. If you're starting with here's the thing, you're way ahead of
them. They're still back here with all this stuff.
What they buy isn't why they buy. When they buy a seminar, and it doesn't
matter how serious it is, doesn't matter if it's 90 minutes or if it's three days,
none of that matters. When they buy a seminar they're buying a seminar. So
what is a seminar? Well, it's basically a learning experience, right? It's adult
education. If they buy a conference, what is a conference or a convention?
Well, it's like a business gathering where people in the same industry or the
same field come together and exchange information and ideas and maybe go
to the exhibit hall. So that's what they're buying. It is not why they're buying
at all; we want to deal with why they're buying.
Different people are nudged over by different things, but there are basically
six or seven different categories of motivations for buying and coming to an
event. So the first is really the fun stuff. And they might not enunciate it or
admit it, but it's really important to most people that they're going to have a
great entertainment experience. They're coming for the cocktail party story
they're going to take home. They're coming for the photo opportunities.
They're coming to see whoever your Shamu the whale is.
And just as an aside, Shamu the whale money is almost always money well
spent. Now it helps. It's beneficial, and in our case we work very hard at it
managing to find a Shamu, a celebrity, who is also relevant, who also has
something useful to say. That's a tough criteria. It's not always possible, but
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	17© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 20 of 60 --

it's also not always necessary. So sometimes relevance doesn't matter, but if
it's somebody cool that people want to see and people want to get a chance to
meet, and people want to get their picture taken with that can be enough. You
can stretch relevance to just barely there.
When we did the borrowed from Yanik the year we did the spy thing, we
used Robert Vaughn from the Man from Uncle, and he had nothing relevant
to dental practices to say. It didn't matter. The theme of the whole thing was
spy, he was a spy, people 45 plus, Man from Uncle, and it’s cool to see him.
That was enough. But Shamu the whale money spent is generally money
well spent. So that category is the fun stuff.
Then there's the category of getting together. So a lot of event attendance is
really driven by fraternity. It's a place to get back together with a bunch of
people you know, but who are scattered all across the country, who you enjoy
seeing, being with, exchanging ideas with, hanging out with, and this is a
convenient time and a place to do it at your event.
Now the key to having that happen is to make sure you make it happen in the
preceding event. So this builds on itself. Fraternity forms again and then
strengthens and strengthens and strengthens. So you've got to make sure
you're creating enough opportunities for people to mix and meet and form
fraternity if you want fraternity to be a driving force for the next event. Ideas
and information sharing all falls into that category.
The next category then is activities. What they do while they're there. And
what's most important to understand about that is even though perhaps we
should want to come sit in a chair, never move from the chair for as many
hours as we can stay awake, and collect as much information as we possibly
can, that does not motivate most people. I will tell you there was a time you
could sell that.
A decade or so ago I used to sell that pretty aggressively. No frills, no
entertainment, no fluff, no food, no nothing, sleep before you get there, don't
plan on sleeping while you're there, we're going to start at dawn, we're going
to go to midnight, we've got a lot to -- I used to sell that. It's not a good thing
to sell now. It's ten years later; the public is just not there. They just are not
there.
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	18© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 21 of 60 --

So activities, a range of activity, so people like to know that there's going to
be different things going on, that they're going to have options. It's why
breakout session choices are good; roundtables where they get to go around
and talk to different experts are good. That they're going to have some
freedom outside the classroom some time. And some people are motivated
purely by the escape.
In the chiropractic profession years ago we used to have a saying about the
chiropractic seminars that we gave. We used to jokingly inside call them
alibis are us because for a certain number of people they were buying an alibi
all the way down to some of them who checked in and were never seen again
until closing ceremonies. Okay by me. That $5,000 spends just as good as
anybody else's $5,000. If you want an alibi I'll sign right here. You bet. He
was in a seminar the whole time. Right -- right here. Just run his card again
because he's not going to question the double charge. No, I'm kidding.
But some people are really motivated by an excuse, a reason, an acceptable
reason to somebody else to get out of Dodge and know that they're going to
go get out of their daily routine, get out of their business, so that's important.
So the fourth category has to do with place, and again, the conversation of
location is a long, complex conversation. I know Bill's going to address some
of it tomorrow night. I mean, personally again, I wouldn't put one in Vegas.
There are certain industries where definitely the place to put it is Vegas
because it dramatically increases attendance. If you have a lot of families, if
you have a lot of mom and pops in your niche, Orlando's a great place to put
it because they can now tag Disney on before or after. If you're putting on a
national convention of strip club owners, Orlando probably not the place.
Most of them are coming with all but the wife and kids. So Vegas is probably
the place.
Place matters. I happen to be a believer in isolation. Not everybody is. So
quite candidly, if it were me you wouldn't be here because you're too close to
a bunch of restaurants and bars and stuff that you can walk to. Not what I
usually do. I want you stranded on my island and it very inconvenient for
you to go anywhere else.
Hotels. Ideally there's only one central bar, not ten bars, because you want
everybody running into each other, but from the marketing standpoint, from
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	19© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 22 of 60 --

the standpoint of telling a story, place matters and not only the physical
location, but what the place is going to be like matters.
Exhibitors. A reason why a lot of people go to conferences, conventions,
seminars, is to find vendors. The exhibitor is a legitimate activity, it's a
legitimate value added, and you ought not necessarily eliminate it.
Resources and tools, gifts and prizes. People like to get stuff. They like to go
home with stuff. They really like to get stuff free. So that can be a big plus.
Next big category is a place to be important. Even successful business
owners, successful professionals, generally speaking are not getting a lot of
applause at home. No matter how well they're paying everybody and how
well their business is doing, when they come in on Monday morning
generally speaking, the staff does not line up, applaud and give them a
plaque. It doesn't happen very often. I've had as many as 48 employees, had
one for a long time, I actually do occasionally, I don't get any applause from
Vicky, but every once in a while she sends me a dirty joke. But, well I mean,
it's something, you know. But I can't recall any of them ever lining up
applauding. It doesn't happen.
So most people are not getting it at home, they’re not getting it in their
business, they are not getting recognition. They are not necessarily being
treated as important, and so a place where they get to come and be an
important V.I.P. They get to wear the better ribbon, and they get to be in the
V.I.P. lounge, or they get to come up on stage and get an award, or they get
recognized from the front of the room. They get pointed out as a seasoned
veteran and then a bunch of other people go ask them questions and that
makes them feel smart. That's a reason why people come. Now, you can't
say it exactly the way I just said it when you're selling it, but you'd better
make sure they're aware of it as part of the total experience.
I wanted to talk just for a minute about the issue of the institutionalized event.
So there are pros and cons to this. Many of you have them because perhaps
you've copied us. The association market of course has them. So this is the
annual convention, or the annual conference that reoccurs and everybody
knows it reoccurs, and it happens pretty much at the same time every year.
So it's an institutionalized event.
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	20© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 23 of 60 --

In Glazer-Kennedy of course the SuperConferencesm is that, and the Info-
SUMMITsm is that. They occur every year and they occur pretty much at the
same time every year. Although they get changed up a lot, fundamentally the
format and the purpose is the same. When this works it works marvelously
well. So in chiropractic for many years when I was doing a lot of work in
chiropractic, I spoke for three years I think in a row, it still exists in a thing
called Parker. Do we have any chiropractors? How many of you are still
going to Parker, like it's the thing to go to? Okay, one, two, not as many.
There was a time when if you were a chiropractor, you went to Parker every
year. That's what chiropractors did. And chiropractors would be asking each
other three months before, you going to Parker? You're going to Parker,
aren't you? Going to Parker. I'll see you at Parker. I mean it's what you did.
In speaking in the National Speakers Association, if you're an NSA member
you go to the convention, that's what you do. And you're weird if you're not
going. So Parker would have thousands of chiropractors there, and they all
went every single year, and they really didn't have to do a hell of a lot of
marketing. They'd put out a brochure and here's who's going to be here, and
everybody went because they're supposed to go. It's what you do if you are
one of them.
When that works it's like gold. Gold. Because there's a predetermined
expectation at the time the event is announced that you're going. When it
doesn't work, it's really bad. So there are pros and cons to trying to make it
work that you should be aware of.
The pros are of course that people know they are supposed to attend. There's
peer pressure to attend. I'll see you there. You're going – “what do you mean
you're not going? How could you not be going? Everybody's going.” So
there's peer pressure to attend.
There's reoccurring fraternity. So everybody knows everybody's going to be
there, and so the buddies that I've got that are not really buddies because we
live at opposite ends of the country, we're going to see each other there. So
most are preinterested and leaning toward registering has the first reaction to
the promotion.
So instead of starting with all this resistance, they're actually starting nearly
predetermined to register because it's the thing you're supposed to do, it's the
thing everybody does, and peers are going to think less of me if I'm not there.
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	21© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 24 of 60 --

It also lends itself to a long sales cycle, which allows you if you wish to do
monthly installments and make the monthly payment pretty low because you
can stretch it over six or seven months.
There are a few information marketers who have one institutionalized
annualized event who literally have people on permanent continuity for the
event. So they're just getting charged every month and they're coming to the
event every year and that's that, unless somebody squeals stop.
The negatives to this approach are, first of all, it's easy for it to drift into same
old, same old. It's easy for the person putting it on, the company putting it on,
to get a little lazy about making it fresh and making it exciting every year
because there's this expectation that everybody's supposed to come and
everybody's going to come. You're vulnerable to the new entry into the
market that has a fresh face and fresh ideas and is very seductive because
they've got something new.
You can find yourself under significant up the ante pressure year from year to
year, so if you've got Shamu this year, who's your bigger Shamu next year?
And then you've got to have three Shamus and pretty soon you've got to have
a whole damned Sea World, and so there's the up the ante pressure. There's
the enormous temptation to say I'll just skip this year because I know it occurs
every single year. And it can be tough to get early registrations because
everybody tends to wait till the last minute, it's hard to convince them that
there's a shortage because they know you do it every year and everybody
shows up. So those are the pros and cons of the institutionalized event.
Sort of the last thing I have for you before we run out of time, is to talk a little
bit about who attends. Well, one kind of person who attends is often the
brand new guy. The newbie. The customer, it's their first event obviously for
everybody it's a first event at some point. They've got to come to their first
one before they can come to the second one. The big obstacle is if it's close
to their first purchase they think they're not ready.
A strategy is to have some sort of special activities just for the newbies. So
for several years in a row with the Info-SUMMITsm, we had the day before
the A to Z Blueprints Day, which now has been replaced with online training,
but if you were new you came to that extra day and you got your orientation
so you were up to speed with everything by the time the event started. It can
be that, can be a first-timers reception, but if you're going to have a lot of
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	22© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 25 of 60 --

newbies, if you have a lot of first-timers, if you want a lot of first-timers, the
strategy is something special for them.
The long-time customer but first-time attendee. So if you have a lot of people
in your customer base, if you're an established info marketer, you've been at
this for a while and you've done umpteen events and you have a bunch of
customers who are good customers, they bought from you, they'll buy from
you again, but you can never get them to an event, you've got to address
reasons why this person who appears to be happy, buys product again and
again, but never comes to an event. Why they haven't come? The gimmick is
again, some sort of special activities and/or special recognition just for them.
So advance sessions that only customers who've been with you for five years
or longer can go to. Some special recognition, longevity awards, etc.
The lost inactive customer ought not to be ignored. Many, many info
marketers are able to get people who dropped out of continuity, they haven't
bought a product in three years, four years, five years, they're like lost
customers, are able to reactivate them with a great event.
Two things you need to know. One, you've got to give them amnesty.
Meaning they can't feel any kind of embarrassment or shame or anything
about returning to the fold after having left.
Secondly, even more important, a good gimmick is to give them for free what
they don't want to pay for as the bonus with attending the event. So for
example, if they've dropped out of continuity and they sort of refuse to stay in
continuity, go ahead and give them a year or two of whatever you're
delivering in continuity as the bonus when they register for the event. They
don't want to pay for it but they'll take it for free.
Practitioners of the slight edge. They are the ones who come to everything.
They are the ones who buy first generally; they're usually the most successful
people you have in your herd. There's a closed loop of that behavior. They
are really slight edge people. They are saying to themselves, if I can just get
one thing it's okay. If I'm there for three days and I just get one thing that's
really useful that I can go use if I tighten a screw here or there in my thing,
that works out to a lot of money, I'm happy. So the best thing to do with them
is go to them first before you go to anybody else and give them recognition
for who they are.
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	23© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 26 of 60 --

Serious students. A very small percentage of almost every group, they also
tend to be the most successful. They really are interested in content, so to
them you've got to make a content story. The ungenerous way to describe
them is seminar junkies, meaning they're the guy that checks into the Hilton
and just stays there and goes from one seminar to the next. Or, the more
pleasant way is they're at least a year's every year.
A, you've got to make it a point of pride to do that. You want to encourage
that behavior with recognition; and B, you still have to figure out a way to
make each event, each reoccurring event feel like it's new and fresh and
different. I worked for a number of years where we did a three and a half day
event every weekend, and I had to get $800 - $900 1,000 people, bought two-
thirds of them the same every weekend. So every Monday we're thinking of
how to describe Friday as something entirely new and different and
groundbreaking than the one everybody just walked out of. Every one was a
groundbreaking, never to be repeated, never seen again, event. It's good
pressure. You learn from the pressure.
Another type of people that come, social animals. So they really are there --
you may not even be able to keep them in the room. They are really there for
the social, the fraternity, the activity. Be sure you give it to them, and let
them know ahead of time that they're going to have those opportunities.
There are the customers who are coming, who are trying to figure it out.
They know they don't get it, they're hoping the light bulb's going to go off,
they're not able to implement, they know they haven't been able to implement,
our strategies for them are again, to acknowledge that they exist and to
promise them to solutions to that particular problem. That they're going to
get blueprints, they're going to get roadmaps, they're going to meet vendors
who can do it for them. They're going to have Q and A opportunity. They're
going to have hot seats. Somehow they're going to get one on one mentoring.
They're going to get to go sit at tables with experts and ask their questions so
they're coming to try and figure it out, you've got to make sure they're going
to have an opportunity to figure it out.
The last ones that come are the ones who are going down for the third time
and reaching up for a life preserver. They're in real trouble. They're in
financial trouble probably. Their businesses are not doing well, and they're
looking for the magic rescue. A) If you have one, be sure you promised it.
B) You want to tell turnaround and rescue stories in your marketing because
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	24© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 27 of 60 --

they're there. Don't ever think by the way that the person who can't afford it
won't buy it and come. I'm going to look over here so I don't make eye
contact with anybody because I don't know. But I promise you in this room,
there are at least a few who absolutely cannot afford to be here, but they're
here anyway. Now, hopefully they do the right thing as a result of having
been here and they never have to suffer that again. I have no control over
that, only they do, but I promise you they're in the room. I promise you they
had to run it on eight credit cards, they had to go borrow it from their mother-
in-law, and they had to sell something to be able to be here. Don't ever think
they're not in your herd, and don't ever think they won't come. And so they
need to be addressed too. And so if you have great turnaround testimonials, if
you have great rescue testimonials, if you have even one dramatic human-
interest story of the person who was one step away from eviction and suicide
and they scraped together bottle caps in order to get to your thing, then tell
the story. And tell the story every time by the way even if you're sick of it
because they're out there and they need to hear it.
So finally, think of the instant reactions that happen when you arrive to offer
somebody a seminar. There's some small percentage who say that they're
waiting. Oh, boy, great, where do I sign up? Then there's some who want to
go, but they've got to justify it to themselves, they've got to justify it to
somebody else. There's some who are cautiously interested. They're like, if
you've ever tried to feed a squirrel in your backyard, you know, you can't
make any fast moves, that's where they are.
There's some who are instantly, probably not, I'm not going to go. I'm not
going to go this year. I'm not going to -- but, I'll listen to the pitch. I'll come
on the teleseminar. Even though I'm not going, I'll come on a teleseminar.
All right, so there are some of them. There's some who are, oh no, not
another seminar, and then there's some who instantly no, I can't, I shouldn't, I
won't, he or she won't let me, I promised him I won't go to another one. So
there's this wide range of instant reactions when you arrive and say, come to a
seminar. And, you have to address all of them because we don't have any
interactivity.
I've got like seven minutes left, so we might as well use it. I am going to do
Q&A.
Audience: All right. So we are not our customers, and...
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	25© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 28 of 60 --

Dan: Well, maybe you are and maybe you aren't, I don't know.
Audience: Okay. And you've been talking about achieving -- getting into
their minds and then being able to write and structure stuff to answer what
they're thinking about, so if we are not our customers and if we do not think
the way they do, how do we achieve that mindset?
Dan: Well, you either know them very well, or you now have to go gather
that. That's why selling cold, selling seminars cold is very difficult. I mean,
the best thing is when you're dealing with a market so, he sent up a gift to me
so he must be here. Is T.J. Rohleder here? Is he here somewhere? T.J.? No.
He sent a gift up to me upstairs so he has to be here somewhere. So, T.J., do
you know him?
Audience: Yeah.
Dan: Okay. So T.J. writes for the blue collar opportunity seeker market. So
you know Al Bundy on Married with Children? Okay. So Al Bundy, but 15
years older is Rohleder's customer. T.J. hits it better than anybody I've ever
seen because he was an opportunity seeker answering all the ads, buying all
the courses, buying all the systems, trying one business after another, and he's
never lost his connection with them. He has them. And so he writes like he's
hiding under their kitchen table, and hearing their conversation at midnight.
Halbert, you could not beat a Halbert control. I don't know anybody that ever
did when he was writing to "every man." Ron LeGrand. So Ron brought into
platinum one time five ads, laid them on the table, and said which one do you
think will work better? And all the platinum members are looking at them
and I picked the right one, but not actually because I thought it was, if I hadn't
known it was a Halbert ad, I would have picked one of the other ones because
it didn't seem like the best ad, but I recognized his Halbert style. Nobody's
going to beat him because he connected with that person.
So the best thing is if you were one of them, or you really live among the
tribe and are sensitive enough to really know where they are. Failing that
you've got to do focus groups, you've got to have conversations, you've got to
go hang out where they are, you've got to pay attention to them, but you really
-- this is about where they are, not where you or I are or what we want to sell
them.
NOTES

-- 29 of 60 --

Audience: Okay. Cool.
Dan: Don't anybody else walk up. We're going to do you and that's going to
be it. Okay. Now this time you got in here legal, right? Well, this is my idea
of entrepreneurial initiative, so we were having the Cleveland Chapter

security to bring him there. And so I'm walking in the paddock and there he
is, which he is not supposed to be, so I consider that admirable personally.

assume that you got in here -- go ahead.
Audience: Thank you. As you're addressing each of these points of
resistance, how do you decide when to address it in the sales process?
Dan: Well, it's a very good question for which there is no good answer. First
of all, you've got to try and rank them in the way that you think they will be
most important to most of your prospects. All right? So which is the biggest
piece of resistance, which is the second biggest piece of resistance. And then
you've got to find places to naturally get to them. So it's not so much we're
going to do them all on page 3. It's not so much we're going to do them after
we cover this.
So it's not that simple. It's where do they fit in the story that we're telling.
Right? That might be right at the beginning. It might be right at the end. It
might be somewhere else, and it might be more than once addressed in
different ways. So we can address them through testimonials. We can
address them through a story. We can address them directly with sales copy.
We can do multi-media, so some of them might be dealt with in print, some
of them might be dealt with on audio, but you generally don't want to hold up
a sign and say here's the 12 kinds of resistance -- but, sometimes you come
close to that. The four reasons why people won't attend and it's not as simple
as where do they go.
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	27© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 30 of 60 --

Audience: Question is: how does this dynamic change if the event is free? I
host an event a couple of times a year, from between 100 to 200 people
register, but the event itself is free. There is no admission fee.
Dan: What a great question. No one should ever think it's easier to sell free
than it is to sell paid. And so many of us use free seminars in one way or
another. A preview, a free teleseminar, a free webinar, and often the
assumption is well, because it's free we don't -- no. For whatever
combination of reasons, putting their butt in the seat for free for 500, for
5,000 all the same dynamics exist. Now, the money resistance isn't there, but
all the other resistances are there. So the dynamics really don't change at all.
Free does not necessarily make it easier. No, you don't get a follow up
because now I'm over time, so you and then we're done.
Audience: Yup. I'll be quick. In my industry the prospects have a lot of
faith in the association, so there's an association with just 4,000 members,
they do a direct mail campaign, and they fill up a room with 700 people. My
question is as an information marketer, other than starting my own
association perhaps, how can I come close to getting that level of trust?
Dan: Well, I'm going to tell you several things real quick. One is you might
consider looking for some sort of relationship with the association.
Sometimes that can be done, sometimes it can't be done. Associations these
days are very much in need of what are called non dues revenue, and so
money does talk, and if in fact you're correct and they are beloved and highly
respected and all that, they may be worth renting in one way, shape, or form.
The other thing I'll tell you is that in most cases, what is true about an
association membership as a market is there's some percentage that are
actually completely dissatisfied and frustrated with what they are getting from
the association and a very opposite positioning will resonate with them. Now
it will repel others, but it will really resonate with them. Only you can figure
that out in your situation, but sometimes that's the play.
Hey folks, hope we had a good night. I gave you as much as I can, thank you
very much.

from the moment you start talking to them, so it's just really figuring out

So here, number one is the seven chief factors that affect seminar
attendance. Here we go. Actually we're doing this as the sort of David
Letterman format, we'll be counting backwards.
Number seven is mindset of the list. So what is the mindset of the list? In
the last couple of years given the down economy, the truth of the matter is in
many cases it's been harder to get people to come to seminars. Most of the
people that I personally consult with and are running seminars, they are
finding it more difficult to fill their seminars because the down economy is
very much on people's minds, so they're saying, “Gee, maybe I shouldn't be
spending the money to come to seminars.” The truth of the matter is it's
probably more important in a down economy to actually find ways to
improve your business or also ways of getting to know about that you could
be improving your business, or finding the business within the business, but
that doesn't change the fact that people do have a mindset of why they
should not be coming to seminars from different times of the year. So
number seven factor is the mindset of the list of your prospects that you
were actually communicating with.
Number six is marketing funnel. Most people underestimate the difficulty of
the job and their marketing funnel is way too small in order to get people to
come to events at Glazer-Kennedy. Just this event alone, over 90 different
marketing touches. Many of them are online that weren't as costly. Some
of them are offline. Some of the offline stuff isn't as costly, some of it is
very, very costly, but over 90 touches.
So most people don't begin to do enough marketing to get people to come to
those events. Quite frankly, we do that battle every day with our
independent business advisors. I know in the room there's many, many
NOTES

-- 33 of 60 --

great independent business advisors, but we do that battle every day with
our independent business advisors to get people to come to their local
chapters. They don't begin to do enough marketing. There's not enough of
a marketing funnel. And with that, there's two things that affect that
dramatically which is diversity of the media. So not just using one media.
If you're just using one line, you're crazy. If you're just using email
marketing online, you're crazy. So it's a diversity of the media.
And the second time is lead-time. Are you giving yourself sufficient
amount of time to do the marketing? We typically at Glazer-Kennedy,
when we go into full force in marketing an event, we give ourselves six
months lead time in order to do the job the right time. Six months we think
in terms of. And those of you that I'm looking around the room that I have
done some consulting with have heard me talk about this over and over
again, is not doing enough of it.
Also looking around the room -- I don't want to point anybody out so I'm
trying not to make eye contact. -- those of you in the room as I look around
who do enough diversity, give themselves enough lead time and do enough
marketing, they have very successful events, and they've learned that. So
there's that point of a fact, the marketing funnel itself, and the two key
factors are diversity, not putting all your eggs in one basket. And number
two is your lead-time to fulfill it.
Number five is size of the list. Obviously the most people that you got that
you can invite, the better chance you've got. It's a numbers game. So the
size of the list. So everything sort of always moves back down to at the end
of the day to build the list. We're all in the list building business. We
should all be in the list building business for many reasons that we should
always be building our list. Constantly. And including the fact that you
always will lose prospects and customers so you have to constantly replace
them as well. So there's list building, which is a reason why tomorrow we
are doing the herd-building day to teach you ways to build a list, and also to
care for the list.
Number four is knowledge about the list. And this is really important
because this allows you to do segmentation. The best result that you can get
in any marketing that you do is the result that the person who's receiving
your message says to themselves, “Hey, this person is speaking to me.”

-- 34 of 60 --

This person is speaking directly to me. So there is seminar attendees versus
non-attendees. That's one way to think about segmenting your list.
Number two is customers versus prospects. Whether they bought from you
before, have never bought from you before. Location of people on the list.
So are they close to where the event is or are they coming from far
distances? Contact information that we have about them. Do we have their
email address, their airmail address? Can we text them? Can we fax them?
Can we call them by phone? Pick up the phone and call them. The more of
that we have, the more media that we can actually use.
Previous purchase history. So what do we know about them? Have they
bought a product in the past? So for example, if somebody were to go our
resource center and buy the A to Z Blueprint seminar. They are raising their
hand and they are saying the fact that I'm interested in information
marketing. So those people should be getting a separate message, which in
our case they do, a separate message telling them that, hey, we notice that
you invested in this. Obviously you're interested in information marketing.

information marketers come to once a year, then this is for you. So knowing
about their purchase history make sit infinitely easier to get people to say
gee, this person is speaking to me.

when we get everything out to you. And you'll see, it has a new whole coat
of paint on it versus anything that we've ever done before. And so therefore,
once again, is making a relevant topic or theme that is different than before
giving it a reason for existence is so important.
Number two is being influential. If those of you who do not have Dan's

actually the Influential Writing and Communication Workshop, teaching
you how to be a person of influence. The job of the sale in any sale that you
have becomes infinitely easier if you are someone who is influential, where
somebody wants to be with you because of the fact that you are someone
who is influential. So being influential is important. And everything that
we do at Glazer-Kennedy is all built on that platform of being people of
influence.
Number one is relationship with a list. Does and would the list know you?
Do they trust you? If they know and trust you, that is the number one thing
you have to have. And the reason why I say that is because I'm always
seeing how people try to go into the seminar business without doing all this
stuff. What they do is they figure this is, you know, they come into a room
like this here and they see 800 people here, and they do the math, so

-- 36 of 60 --

somebody on average paid $2,000 to be here so $2,000 times 800 and they
say, “Gee, this is an easy business. I'm going to just do this business.”
They don't realize what it takes to do that. So therefore you have to really
earn the right -- and I've written articles about that -- earn the right to be
able to sell things to people. And that's really what this is all about; earning
the right, and having the relationship with the list and being influential are
the two key factors that gives you earning the right in order to ask people to
do business with you.
