and deploy. So the last little category is cool stuff. With that said, let's get
started to repurpose with power.
What do I mean by repurpose with power? What do we do at Glazer-
Kennedy, and what do I suggest that you do? What we do at Glazer-
Kennedy is we start with our main piece of marketing. We start with our
main piece of marketing and we always build that. Quite frankly, that is the
biggest job that we do in our marketing is we first start with our main piece
of marketing. How do we do that? We build a multi-page sales letter that
we put everything in there. Everything in this multi-page sales letter. And
from that multi-sales page letter we repurpose everything out of that.
So for example, here is the front cover of a sales letter that I think was for
the two years ago Info- SUMMITSM. The very front cover, you've got this,
it was personalized with the person's name, “Open immediately, define how
you can attend a $5,000 copywriting boot camp with Dan Kennedy and Bill

Glazer for free.” And then the whole front cover of the newsletter with
Suzanne Somers, who was at that time our celebrity entrepreneur that we

Schaefer. Then we had Timothy Seward speak and we talked about him, and

complete copy of everything that I show you here at the end, so you're not
going to have time -- I don't have time to read it all because I want to get as
much content in in these 90 minutes as I can. But it's a full sales letter.
So then how do we repurpose that? Then after that, there's success stories,
and there, now this is the website -- purpose. So this was the page you came
to at the website and you just sort of identified yourself by your level of
membership. So you clicked on the ticket that identified the level of
membership, and then it went right to the same sales letter that I just showed
you that was offline. So we're rolling out the red carpet. And this when
online, you'd scroll on down all the way to the end of the sales letter that I
just showed you offline, we repurposed it for our online sales letter.
Today we do a lot of video sales letters as all of you probably have seen.
Our video sales letters are repurposed. I take the same content that I do in
my big main sales letter and I actually turn that into a video sales letter
where the text comes up on screen as somebody is actually reading it. So
it's the exact same thing. How do I do that? What I do is I take my main
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	35© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 38 of 60 --

sales letter or the main printed sales letter, and I get out two really valuable
instruments that are hard to find, but you can find if you search enough for
them, it's a ruler and a pen. Those two things. And I put lines under each
section that I want to cut out to make a message that I use. So like for
example, that is my online sales letter, then I start moving to emails and I'll
take the sales letter and I'll chop it into, in this particular case, 18 emails. I
took my main sales letter and I chopped it into 18 emails that I distribute
over usually six weeks. Over six weeks we'd send out 18 emails, each one
with a piece of -- that came right out of the main sales letter. So that's how I
repurpose it.
What's important about that, why I tell you that, is because everybody thinks
the job of writing the copy, they make it harder than it needs to be. The job
of writing the copy is to create your main big sales letter. Main, main big
sales letter. Then after you do that, then you repurpose it and use it as many
ways as you possibly can. Everybody get that? Got it? Yes. Right.
So it really isn't as difficult as you think it is. It is difficult to write that
main sales letter, but once you got that the rest of it is now just repurposing
it and moving forward with it. So there's a couple of emails that are
chopped out of there.
The second thing is celebrity. Now Dan talked about it last night and I just
can't echo it enough. Most people are like really cheap about this and that's
really poor thinking. What did he call him last night? Shamu the whale,
right? Shamu the whale. And I couldn't wait to call Cal and tell him he was
Shamu the whale... And Ivanka Trump loved being known as Shamu the
whale.
But that's really what this is all about is you want to get somebody who’s
that person in themselves, although they're expensive. Cal did not come here
for free as much as I'd like him to have done that. George Foreman never
came for free. Ivanka didn't come for free. Gene Simmons was the most
expensive person I have ever gotten to come to the Glazer-Kennedy stage
and he was major expensive. None of these people came for free, but it
didn't matter to me because it all comes down to by them being there, how
many more seats do I have to sell to justify them being there? And in all
cases, they will justify that expense.
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	36© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 39 of 60 --

Gene Simmons without a doubt put 200 people into the Glazer-Kennedy
seminar the year he spoke by him being there because people came that
would never normally have come to a Glazer-Kennedy seminar. We know
that for a fact, and then many of them once they experience Glazer-Kennedy
seminars they'll keep coming back over and over and over again. So the
celebrity is a key thing, and you really want to be thinking in terms of
finding the right celebrities, but also at the same is relevant celebrities.
So as Dan Kennedy said last night we don't just get a celebrity, we also
bring one that has relevancy. So always looking for somebody who's an
entrepreneur celebrity as well. So for example, the year that we brought --
well, you know it's Three's Company, you know so we just photo shopped,
thank God for Photo Shop, Suzanne Somers was coming so we photo
shopped Dan and by the way, please notice Dan's right arm and the
placement of it, but that was how we used it. Now that does the same thing
that the celebrity on the infomercial at 2:00 in the morning when you are
turning channels and you see a celebrity makes you stop and say, what is
whoever celebrity's on at that time, what is this person doing on the air at
2:00 in the morning? What this person does, the male or female celebrity, is
it makes you stop changing the channels and stop and look at it. That's the
same thing the celebrity does is what is Suzanne Somers doing with Dan
Kennedy and Bill Glazer? What is Suzanne Somers doing at a Glazer-
Kennedy event? It is something that makes you stop and look at the
marketing. So that was the one that we used with Suzanne Somers.
This is one that Rory Fatt used, Famous Amos, the chocolate chip cookie
guy. It's a perfect fit for him. He's in the restaurant business so it's all about
food. Celebrity makes you stop and look.

whenever we can. We're entering the conversation that's in their head

around holiday times and sending out a holiday card, which is one of the

for example, this is a cover letter that went out that we used, we put it on top

might be wrong, but I think it was the Influential Writing Workshop, the

based on what we know about them to invite them to come to an event that
it's a clearly targeted message to them. So this “screw surviving” cover
letter that we had about eight different versions of this depending on what
your previous buying history was, everybody got the letter but it had
different copy in it depending on what you did in the past.

there's a little toy car and we mail this out to people that are within driving
distance of the event, and we say copy in there along the lines of because of
the fact people are coming from far places such as Japan and Australia and
New Zealand and the United Kingdom, and they're spending thousands of
dollars to fly here, just imagine the fact that you can just get into your car
and drive on over to the seminar. So we have a special opportunity for you
because you live within driving distance. That is list segmentation, which
again, by the way I'm not going through everything with you as far as
events, these pieces, but again, on the CD that you can get at the end,
everything is on there for you to actually get swiped and deployed for your
businesses. If I went through each one with you we would be here for like
three days, so I'm just giving you the highlights of it.

This is another one. These are for people that live outside the United States.

that's printed on the outside of it. This is a special message that goes to
people who live outside the United States. This is list segmentation and it
makes a big, big difference in how people respond.
This one is an interesting one in the list segmentation department, which is
actually an endorsed mailing. So like for example, this is a mailing that
Infusion mailed out to all their customers saying to them, “because you are a
customer of Infusion, I'd like to invite you to come to the seminar that I'm
speaking at,” which in this particular case Clate Mask was speaking at, and
also at that seminar we did a special training for Infusion users. That's a
specialized, segmented mailing that we mailed out to other folks.
So again, the question that you need to be thinking to yourself is what
information do I know about my list? How can I slice and dice this
information in order to make it relevant to the thing that I am actually
offering folks?
Again, I just want to tell you something, if I was up here right now and not
talking about information marketing, not talking about seminar marketing,
and if I was talking to a group of restaurant owners, if I was talking to a
group of mortgage brokers, if I was talking to a group of dentists, God
forbid I was talking to a group of lawyers, all those things together, I would
be teaching them the same thing. The way you win the battle is by matching
your message to your specific market so that people say to themselves, hey,
this person is speaking to me.
Next is diverse media. And again, I have a lot of -- in my mastermind
groups, I have a lot of these guys that are pure Internet marketers. Thank
God not all of them, but a lot of them. They do outnumber the real estate
investing guys, so -- especially in this economy. But I have a lot of them in
there. And I had this battle with them all the time. They want to completely
win the battle of filling their seminars with just sending out emails. And I'm
telling you that the ones that listen to me, they get this that they don't just
send out emails.
So I'm looking over there, Lloyd Irvin's sitting over there. So Lloyd doesn't
have this problem. Lloyd is very well recognized for lots of things, but one
of the things he's well recognized for is the fact that he's a very, very bright
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	39© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 42 of 60 --

internet marketer. But when you listen to everything that he does -- he just
did a seminar in the martial arts business, he used all kinds of media, not
just online stuff. See, he doesn't fall prey to that. The ones that use
diversity in the media, they always will win this battle over the people that
just use one kind of media. I'm picking on email marketers, could just be
direct mail.
You don't want to just use one thing so let me show you a couple of
examples. First of all, here's newsletters inserts. By the way, this should be
familiar to you, this is a repurposed product. We took the main sales letter
and pulled out of there segments that we put as newsletter inserts in our No
B.S. Marketing Letter, and also some of our other newsletters each month.
It's a re-purposed product, but we put inserts in our newsletters. That's
diversity.
Here's another one, our online TV show, our online media. We promote our
events on our online TV show. Here's another one, which is our No B.S.
Not So Daily Ezine. It's another place that we promote our events. Here's
another one, this is faxing. So this is actually a fax that we sent, a two-page
fax that we send out and we use fax very sparingly because it is the one
media that more people get upset about than any other media that we do, so
we don't overuse it, we just use it sparingly, but when we do use it, it's still
very effective.
By the way, there's a lesson here about the two-page fax that I wanted to
stop and tell you about, which is the fact that page number one is the
message, and page number two is the order form. And it's really important
whenever you do faxing is you always do a second page which includes the
order form. And the reason why is because when you do this, they don't
have the order form in front of them if you don't fax them the order form, so
they now have to go and find the order form and/or they have to go online to
register. You're making it difficult for them. They are at the fax machine.
Fax them two pages, fax them an order form so while they're at that fax
machine they can fill it out and fax it back. Let them use the media that
brought them to the party to actually respond to it. So that's two-page fax.
Whenever you're doing a fax, always use two pages and always send out the
order form with it.
Here's a voice broadcast. Now this is the text, but this is a voice broadcast.
So we use voice broadcast, another type of media. We use it more often
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	40© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 43 of 60 --

than we use faxing, but we don't overuse it. What I like using in voice
broadcast is I like using celebrity impersonators, so I'm not sure which one
this is. Oh, this is Superman. So we actually did the whole script on the
whole Superman thing. Look up in the sky, it's a bird, it's a plane, that kind
of thing. So we like to do things that are kind of cute with it because people
enjoy getting on their answering machines, they enjoy getting more
personality and so that they don't get upset about it, they sort of say that
laugh about it. So again, use outrageous advertising, which I teach in all
your voice broadcasts.

this event, and so he does a post-it note on the outside of the newspaper
talking about the event, and then actually inside he also has an ad in there
that's also talking about the event. So what he usually does, doesn't have to,
they both stand on their own, but he usually has a little post-it note driving
them to the big ad inside the paper. Very, very effective for local events is
use newspaper advertising still and the good news is that it's become much,
much less expensive to use it because those guys are traumatized right now
by being a difficult media. So you can negotiate real hard with those folks.

the enrollment form on the other side of the post card. So this is one side
where he had his message, and on the other side it's the actual form they can
fill out and just send it right back. So it's right on the post card. So post
cards are a good type of media.

four-page magalog and on page 2 there's a sales letter, page 3 there's the rest
of the sales letter, and page 4 there's the rest of the sales letter. So once
again, it's a magalog, it's a different type of media.

But this is sending out a little booklet with a sales letter inside and a CD in
there because some people will listen. I personally, if I had to pick one or
the other as far as which one I would use, I would use CDs over DVDs, if I
had to, but that's only if I had to. What I prefer to do is use them both. I
want to use every type of media I can. Why do I pick CDs over DVDs?
Because it's easier for people to consume CDs than to watch DVDs. It's
because you can put them in your car as you're driving around. In my car,
I've always got a pile of CDs in there, so I'm using it sort of like it's a
traveling university to always have me learn more.

takeaway. If you're looking for something that you want to do that very few
other people are doing, demonstration are really, really important.

When you watch infomercials they are a total demonstration. They're
demonstrating the products that they are selling. It's a demonstration and
any time they do demonstrations of the use of the product, the efficiency of
the product, they always get better sales. And that same thing happens in
seminar stuff, so for example, here's a piece, somebody also talked about the

also gets your attention because you might have won the prize. This is the
puzzle mailing.
This one a lot of people have used, but it's kind of cool. This is “your
tickets are enclosed.” On the outside of the envelope, and what they
actually do, this is for a seminar, which they actually mail you what looks
like very official looking tickets, and so it's actually inside the marketing
piece of the actual tickets to get you in so your tickets are enclosed. So if
you get something in the mailbox that says “your tickets are enclosed,”
you're very likely to open it up and say “Gee, what's this all about? I want
to find tickets.”
This one is Joe Polish's check mailing. So Joe Polish, this is about sending
out a mailing that looks like somebody's mailing you a check. So if
somebody mails you a check, what are you going to do? Of course you're
going to open it up. I particularly like this one because he mails out this
check and the check is for -- can anybody read how much the amount of the
check is for? $42,000 and how much? $42,000. And he has a thing on
there that says “here's how I'm going to spend my money list.” So we're
mailing them out a check for $42,000 and inside there is an insert in there

talking about how you're going to spend the money. So what is the $42,000

that he has makes an extra $42,000 a year in cleaning carpets. That's how
he makes that work. And this is how I'm not going to spend my additional

This is the Jay Squirrel piece. I don't know who invented Jay Squirrel, it's
been around as long as I can, but this is sending out the marketing materials
from Jay Squirrel and some fictitious person that lives in the attic of your
business, and you always attach a thing of peanuts to it from Jay Squirrel,
and it's all about “you must be nuts if you're not taking advantage of this
opportunity.” So that's the Jay Squirrel piece.
And my mother and mother-in-law's here, so I'm just going to say cool stuff.
Believe me, if they weren't there I would speak proper English. So
examples for you to S&D. This is really big. This is a seminar survey to
find out what they want letter. This is a real short cut for a lot of folks if
you want to create a seminar, and I teach this at a lot of different places, this
is basically my three step system that I use and I teach to get whatever you
want sold, sold infinitely easier.
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	45© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 48 of 60 --

Step number one is to survey your list. Step number two is to report back
results to them of the survey. And step number three is to give them what
they want, which in this particular case the results in this case are these are
the things that I wanted people to speak about at the seminar. So these are
the topics that you choose, that you wanted, now we've built the seminar for
you. Now this works in any business. This doesn't have to be in the
seminar business. We use the three-step survey for just about any business
that I now do consulting with, but it works really, really well in seminars.
So you survey the list, you report back results, usually when you report back
results you also give them a gift for filling it out. In this particular case, the
year we did it we gave them a tee shirt, and then we also then did our whole
sales letter built on the premise that says, this is what you asked for so we're
giving you now what you asked for. It's a pretty advanced strategy and
those of you that are looking for advanced stuff, this is a very smart strategy
to use for your seminar marketing.
This is the personalized with don't jump fax. So this is our first name that's
on there with the great cartoon, “wait don't jump.” It's not too late to
register for the event. You always would include this with a registration
form. So it's two-step. But this little cartoon thing that's on there, again,
you all will get it in the file that we have for you. This little cartoon thing
always works best. I've never seen anything out-perform this in a fax.
This is the keep-them-engaged mailing. This is kind of an interesting thing.
So all of you got over the course of the last couple of months from us the
CDs of the set of how successful entrepreneurs think they're real rich, right?
You all got it, right? So it was a six CD set, and every month for three
months you got two CDs, right? And then on the fourth month you also got
a CD of our vendors. So why do we do that? I told you I was going to
reveal stuff that I shouldn't reveal about how I'm manipulating you guys.
We do it because we can keep you engaged. A lot of people register for our
events far in advance, and we want to keep them excited about the event, so
over the course of time, usually about four months out from the event, I start
sending out every month a mailing to everybody who is coming with
information on there about the event, you know, here's how you register for
the hotel. Here's about others, if you want to add a guest, here's how you
add a guest. On and on and on and on and on, plus here's a free gift for you.
And this is all about just keeping them engaged and excited about the events
that they're coming to. So again, advanced strategy for all of you, which is
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	46© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 49 of 60 --

going to make it really tough for me to keep coming up with new stuff if I
keep showing you everything I'm going to do for you.
This one is kind of an interesting thing. This is add on a seminar to a
seminar letter. So, what this is -- this happens to be in 2003 -- no, it was
actually 2004, Dan Kennedy did the Mega Conference in Phoenix. This was
the last Dan Kennedy event before Glazer-Kennedy. Anybody at that Mega

know, I mean, you know, both of you are collecting social security now.
But -- and I was there also. So what this was, was I was running my BGS

on an extra day to Dan's event, because Dan had Mark Victor Hansen
coming, Joe Sugarman, he had a real big lineup - Dan Kennedy himself, so I
tacked on a one-day seminar onto his multi-day seminar and I actually sold
it for an additional amount of money. I had close to 70 people in the room
for my extra day that I had that I actually charged money for. So that is an
add on a seminar to a seminar letter.
Who here has an industry trade -- industry conventions? That's where you
see it most often done is at industry conventions. People who have an
industry convention, you can add on a day. I always tell you to do it the day
before because very few people are left at the end of the events, so do it the
day before. You can have a one-day event before the industry convention in
that city where they can actually come to your one-day before because most
people get in early, but they never stay until the end.
This is another thing. This is kind of a cool thing. A lot of info marketers
do it. We never make the recordings of our events available to anybody, but
this is when you market to your list, and you do a lot of marketing to them,
but they still don't come. That marketing that you've done to them still has a
lot of value because you've softened the beach head to them on the fact that
you're having this big event, but they decide not to come. Normally what
happens is about ten days to two weeks before the event happens, you send
them this sold out piece to the event, but you tell them because we're sold
out you can buy the tapes, the CDs, the DVDs of the event that you're about
to miss. So this is now selling the boot camp in a box of the seminar that
they were invited to come to, but they decided not to come. You will
always, always, always, squeeze a lot of money because of all the marketing
you did to them you've now softened the beach head for that message that
you sent to them.
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	47© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 50 of 60 --

This one actually is a Lloyd and Vicky Irvin letter, which I will tell you is a
direct rip off of something that we wrote, so thank you for that. I mean,
every word that we wrote you ripped me off, so it's terrific. So this is the
follow up to people were coming to an event, but they didn't show up, so in
the local market you see this a lot where they didn't show up. You now send
out this which is an “overwhelming wave of sadness washed over me
piece,” and we actually tell them because you decided you didn't show up, I
wanted to give you a second to come to the -- usually, in a local market you
have them often so you can come to the next one. So this is following up to
the no-shows people that don't come to your events.
Now we're at number 39. So number 39 is the homer letter. I think this is
the last one I got. I showed you -- I told you I have 39 exhibits. This is the
homer letter. This is what happens is that at the best time if you're doing an
event and repeating it every year, the best time to sell that event is at that
event for next year. That is the best time to sell it. When we left our
SuperConferenceSM in 2010 this year, for next year's SuperConferenceSM we
left with 647 people registered for next year. Thank you. That takes a
tremendous amount of pressure off of the promoter to now have to win next
year. I mean, next year, the 2011 SuperConferenceSM I'm pretty certain
we'll 1,200 people there again, but you know what? It's a lot easier to start
with 647 people than it is to start from zero. So the best time to sell it, if
you're doing it again next year or whatever, is to sell it at the actual event
that you are at.
However, not everybody registers. So this is the letter that I've sent out for
everybody who didn't register, it goes out right away. For everybody who
didn't register when they left the event, and I've sent it out from various
people. I've sent it out once from Karen, you know it came from Karen, a
neglected wife. Once it came from Sherrie, my assistant, a beaten assistant.
Once it came from I think John Gibbs also. And this one came from my
dog, Homer.
So this was a letter that I sent out from my dog telling everybody that Bill's
away right after the event, he's relaxing, he's resting, which is total -- not
that I'm back at work Monday morning, but he's away, and before he gets
back, if you still want to register for the event and I can only think of three
reasons why you didn't register. One reason is that you got so caught up in
the event that you forgot to turn in the form.
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	48© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 51 of 60 --

The second reason is -- where are they now? The second reason is because
of the fact that you're afraid of coming back next year because you won't
implement everything that you set out to do and what better way to hold
yourself accountable than now registering for next year's event so you know
that you've got to get it all done?
And the third reason why you don't come back is something that I absolutely
positively cannot remember right now, but when you get everything Larry
will tell you the third reason. I can't remember what it is right now.
So you have to have reason why copy somebody did not register. Oh, the
third reason why is because you left early. You had to leave early for some
unknown reason. You had to get home, and so you weren't there when
people actually offered you the opportunity to come back again the
following year. So there were your three reasons why. So you have reason
why copy. You send that out, and we always get people who did not
register who should have registered to fax in their registration. This is a sort
of insider trick for you all, which is going to make it infinitely easier --
harder for me to keep doing this stuff on you guys in the future.
Now all you have to do in order to just make my job easier, just register
while you're here and then leave me alone.
Seminar logistics. This is the painful, but necessary stuff. The stuff that
Dan Kennedy asked me personally to talk about, and which I hate talking
about it because it is painful, but let's talk about it. One, site selection.
Where you're doing it at. What are the big things to think about? Number
one thing is location. Dan is 100% right. It's best to keep people isolated.
The fact that we are in downtown Baltimore in this beautiful hotel
surrounded by these great restaurants and a beautiful waterfront is not the
ideal location to do it at all. The fact that I can get in my car and ride 20
minutes to be here was a big help for that, but as most people know at
Glazer-Kennedy events, we usually have them at airport facilities where
you're kind of isolated. So location.
Flight access. How easy it is to get a flight in. My favorite place to have an
event not because it's my favorite city, although it is one of my favorite
cities, but my favorite place to have a seminar is actually Chicago because
it's about a two and a half hour flight from anywhere in the United States.
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	49© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 52 of 60 --

It's easy to get to. There's two big airports there. Always lots of flights
coming in and coming out, so it's my favorite place to have it, but you can't
have it there every time, so we move them around. But the flight access.
The best time of year. When is a good time of year to have it? Chicago is
not particularly a good time to have an event in February. So the event that
I'm doing with Matt Bacak, the JV Affiliate event in January, we're having it
in Orlando. Good weather for that time of year.
Site tour. Really important is we have a crew led by Sherrie and before we
sign it on the dotted line that we're going to go to a hotel, we send out
usually two of our people, Sherrie and somebody else to go visit the hotel.
To see the hotel, make sure everything is in good order at the hotel, so do a
site tour. Usually if you have to fly somewhere and your event's large
enough, they will pay for you to come out and do the site tour. It doesn't
cost you any money other than your people's time. And the type of suites
available for VIPs. So we have VIPs that come in, all of our big speakers
and all of that stuff, so we try to get good suites for them, so are they
available? Those are site selection things to consider.
Next is important terms of the clauses. They will not tell you this unless
you ask for it. They hope you don't know anything. They really hope that
you don't. They are the enemy. I can tell you that right now. So Internet
rate clause, cancellation clause if you have to cancel, noise clause. I don't
know if any of you ever been in an event, but one time I think Ron, you
were telling me a story once you were speaking somewhere and the alarm
system went off or something like that. Everything has happened
somewhere where you've spoken at, so noise clause. Hotel concessions.
What kind of concessions the hotels will give you. These are important
terms of your contracts that you've got to really, really negotiate hard.
Next one is hidden fees. Hidden fees they have there. Shipping and
receiving. You have no idea how much they can charge you for shipping.
By the way, did anybody know how much a gallon of coffee is when we
serve it here? $60 a gallon of coffee in this crappy place here where we're
at. What? That's right. Plus all the other extra stuff, so a gallon of coffee. I
mean there's like no conscious at all.
AV requirements. Convention center fees if you're in a convention center.
Types of rooms on a contract. So what are the types of rooms your guests
get? In our particular case, we always negotiate for our attendees to get the
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	50© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 53 of 60 --

nicest rooms that they have. Rigging costs, whether there's any rigging
costs because they will really rip you off unless you negotiate away from it.
You know about the stuff and you actually negotiate it out of your contract.
Hotel bonuses to ask for. The meeting room rental fee. The meeting room
rental fee that you're in right now is directly proportioned to how many
rooms you fill in the hotel. So you can get these rooms oftentimes for free if
you're filling up enough hotel rooms. Twenty-four hour room rental.
Whether or not you have access to the room for 24 hours, or you have
access to it for 12 hours, 16 hours, how long you have access to that room.
Service fees waived. Whether you can get the service fees waived, but
usually if you can't get them waived you can get a portion of them waived.
Resort fees. I love that one. I can't tell you how many seminars I've spoken
at as a speaker and all of a sudden I go to check out and there's a resort fee
on there for like $20 or $25 and all I did was went to the seminar. I didn't
even know they had a resort at the place.
Incentives to register for early room registration. This is kind of an
interesting thing, which we are now working on ourselves is whether if we
can get our attendees to register before a deadline where they get even a
better rate. More and more hotels now are doing that for you.
Vendor tips. Tips that deal with your vendors. Number one is local
exhibitor decorators. You always will do better if you use your local
exhibitor decorator than if you're using some big national firm. So you
always want to try to find them. Usually the hotels are pretty good about
giving you a list of those folks.
The next thing is set up time, break down time during normal hours. When
did we start moving in here at Glazer-Kennedy? The event starts on
Thursday, and when did we send our first crew out? Monday. We start
moving in on Monday. Every day we start adding more and more people
that we're bringing here in order to get ready for this, so you have set up
time and break down time, enough time to actually get this event put
together, or any event that you do. Provide AV bids with agenda and
detailed list of equipment. So there are AV folks. I love dealing with these
guys back here, but we keep them honest. So before we come to any event
we always have a group of folks that we actually give the bid out to and
show them a complete list of all the equipment that we need, so you want to
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	51© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 54 of 60 --

do that as well because there's great diversity, great range that you can
actually find and what people will charge you for that.
Discount from vendors for booking multiple events, and the AV folks will
usually give you discounts for multiple events, the hotels will give you
discounts for multiple events. Marriott, Sheraton, Hyatt, Hiltons, if you say
to them I'm doing three events in the next two years, well, they'll give you
even better discounts for multiple events. So those are some vendor tips.
That was the dreaded stuff. I will tell you in terms of a hotel contract like
this, going back and forth, Sherrie going back and forth with them over and
over back and forth, back and forth, probably takes us to negotiate and
finalize a contract, usually around six weeks with all the back and forth, so
it's not like you just get the hotel contract and you sign it. If you sign the
first hotel contract that they delivered to you and you sign it and send it back
to them, what they do is they get their entire staff in a room and they all start
laughing at you. So what you've got to do is understand that they are testing
you to see how much of this stuff you actually know.
Seminar choreography to maximize profitability. So this is now
choreographing the event. The event -- similar to an event you're sitting in
right now, or any event you go to, you have to look at it as choreographing
it. This is probably the single biggest thing that I do with people that I
consult with in the seminar business where I help them to actually put on
really very, very productive and profitable events. We always have a big
session, never less than an hour to actually figure out how to choreograph
the entire event.
So here's the things we think about. Number one is making sure you deliver
great value as soon as possible. Deliver great value as soon as possible.
Who's the first speaker here yesterday? Dan Kennedy. Did he deliver great
value? How many of you, well I don't won't ask, don't raise your hand,
many of you walked up to me after Dan's presentation and said if I just go
home right now, I'm done. Most of you who didn't walk up to me were
thinking that. Great value after that Cal Ripkin, Jr., Mary Ellen Tribby, I
mean great value early right away to deliver great value, so all of a sudden
people say to themselves I made the right decision to come here. So you've
got to deliver right off the bat great value as soon as possible.
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	52© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 55 of 60 --

Number two. You've got to make the list. Your number one, your number
two, number three chief objectives of the seminar for the host. You've got
to make the list of what your three top objectives are and you've got to now
choreograph the event to put number one first, number two second, number
three third, as far as what you want to do. It is not a goal of a Glazer-
Kennedy event, but a lot of people that I work with in niche markets their
biggest objective is to sell coaching programs at their events. Obviously, it's
not a goal at a Glazer-Kennedy event because we don't sell a lot of coaching
programs. Even my platinum program that I offered earlier today is only for
some of you, it's not for all of you, so it's not our chief goal. But at a lot of
events, the number one thing they want to sell is coaching their events.
That's their number one thing, so when we choreograph the whole agenda,
it's all built around selling that thing and then everything else takes a seat
back to it. Number two takes a back seat to it second, number three third.
Diversity. Non-duplication of topics. This thing is crazy, okay. You
wouldn't believe how many times speakers complain to me that they go to
events and speak at it and there's two other people on the agenda talking
about the same thing they're talking about. It's crazy. So you can't have any
duplication of topics.
Number four is speaker introductions. How the speaker is introduced makes
a gigantic difference in the choreography of the event. Most of us that are
serious about putting on events, we always introduce the speakers ourselves.
Chris? Where's Chris? Chris, you in the room? Chris Cardell. Chris
Cardell is in Guinness Book of Records for being the best speaker
introducer of all times. He's in the book of records for that. He does the
best introductions because I mean, Dan and I flew over to the U.K., they
never heard of Bill Glazer and Dan Kennedy in the U.K. enough, and by the
time that he was done introducing us we were kind of famous over there,
you know. So speaker introductions are really, really important, how you
introduce the speaker. And this also ties into being a person of influence
and also having credibility and believability with your attendees. But the
speaker, I mean the introducer being the person of influence because he can
transfer that influence over to now the speaker. The introducer, the host can
do that.
Speakers being respective of other speakers. Those of you in the room --
how many of you do a fair amount of speaking? A lot of speaking. Okay.
So you had an incidence on this where you've spoken on the stage and other
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	53© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 56 of 60 --

speakers have tried to sabotage you. You know, we had one speaker years
ago, some of you were probably there, it was the most sabotaging speaker of
all times I've ever seen. He has never been back on the Glazer-Kennedy
stage since that time. Really big sabotage. He'll try to do anything at all to
hurt the other speakers. So making sure speakers are being respectable of
other speakers. Have you seen that at this event where you're at? Were all
the speakers respectable to everybody else? Everybody finished on time.
Did you see all that? Nobody going over. Nobody talking badly about
anybody. I won't even tolerate it at our stage, and it's important that that is
thought about. And if you don't know about that, you can get sabotaged by
your speakers.
If the speaker is offering a resource, God forbid, you have to think about
who speaks before the speaker, who speaks after the speaker, this when the
order forms are distributed, how the order forms are collected, what the
speakers average closing percentage is elsewhere, and how speakers
resources affect your number one, number two, and number three objective.
So if you bring in a speaker, will that speaker's resource affect your main
three objectives and what you're trying to accomplish at your events? So all
that has to be thought out in the choreography of the events.
Position of speakers to keep attendance throughout the seminar. So if you
don't have speakers that are speaking throughout the seminar to keep
attendance, so let me just tell you a little bit of inside baseball. Do you
know why Frank Kern is speaking tomorrow and not the first day? That is
because he is positioned in a way because everybody wants to hear Frank
Kern speak, and he's positioned in a way that everybody will be here
tomorrow morning to hear Frank Kern speak on the third day of the event.
Why are we giving away the Cal Ripkin jersey and all the gifts at the end?
That's all about bringing people back. So I'm being totally transparent to
you all, so you can think worse of me now or later, but it doesn't matter, I'm
telling you it's all about keeping people for attendance.
General session versus breakout session topics. So my rule of thumb on that
usually is a general session topic should be interesting to everybody in the
room. And a breakout session should be specialized where people can
choose which ones they want to go to. And there's a real important purpose
for both, but you also have to think of that in terms of do you want to have
general sessions or breakout sessions?
NOTES
BUTTS IN SEATS	54© 2011/Glazer-Kennedy Insider’s Circle

-- 57 of 60 --

Then there's the secret sauce. The secret sauce to these events, and Dan I
was happy to see spoke about this yesterday, is moving from a seminar to a
unique experience. See these are no longer seminars. They really have to
be unique experiences, so the GKIC events unique experience.
Networking events for certain levels of membership. Special meal functions
with certain levels of membership. Book signings with the authors. Meet
and greet and photo opportunities with the celebrities. Contests and
competitions. Award ceremonies. Prize giveaways. Chair massage
therapists. VIP lounge for certain levels of membership. Special Q&A
sessions with certain attendees. Exhibitors as resources. Speaker's
resources to facilitate fast growth. So actually offering speakers' resources
to facilitate fast growth, so the people that we allow to come here and offer
you a resource, it's not just because we want to sell you stuff, it's because of
the fact once we show you that this is something that would help you in
your business and you make that decision whether this is helpful in your
business, you can then invest in something to get you moving fast in it.
So like for example, let's use Stephen Beck's webinar program. Once you
realize that I want to do better webinars, or I want to do webinars, his thing
is all designed in a way to get you doing that faster, and getting you to do
them better, so it's really valuable to have resources, and if you don't have --
it's actually unvaluable if you are able to show people the opportunity but
then not give them the sources they need to implement the opportunity.
There's of course a lot of thought has to be given into that.
So like for example, here is an agenda. So this is an internal agenda. You
probably would not see something like this as far as how we choreograph
our entire events that we use in our seminar so we have the registration the
night before, Thursday and then it moves on to Friday. And there's breakout
sessions that are over there and then we go back to general session and then
that's that. And then over here I think this is then the bonus day. This is
actually the bonus day we had at the 2010 SuperConferenceSM, which was
the greatest bonus day we ever had the whole time we did the big showcase
of people competing for the title and more people have given me great
feedback about that than anything else we've ever done, which is why we're
doing it again next year with our members.
NOTES
